data.title: Assessing Applied Materials (AMAT) After A 108% One Year Share Price Surge
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Applied Materials' share price still reflects good value after its strong run, it helps to step back and look at what the current market price might be implying about the business.
  • The stock last closed at US$354.91, with returns of 7.9% over 7 days, 8.5% over 30 days, 32.0% year to date, and 108.4% over the past year, which naturally raises questions about how much of the story is already priced in and how risk is being perceived.
  • Recent attention around Applied Materials has largely centered on its role as a key supplier to the semiconductor industry and how investor interest in chip related themes is feeding through to equipment makers. That backdrop is important context when thinking about whether the current share price is mainly reflecting enthusiasm for the sector or the company’s own fundamentals.
  • Right now, our valuation framework gives Applied Materials a value score of 3 out of 6, based on how many checks suggest the stock could be undervalued. Next, we will walk through the main valuation approaches before finishing with a way to look beyond simple ratios and models.

Applied Materials delivered 108.4% returns over the last year. See how this stacks up to the rest of the Semiconductor industry.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Applied Materials Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes forecasts of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required rate of return, to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

For Applied Materials, the model here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, using Free Cash Flow (FCF) in $. The latest twelve month FCF is about $7.0b. Analysts provide detailed estimates for the next few years. Beyond that, Simply Wall St extrapolates to build a full 10 year path. By 2030, projected FCF is $11.4b, with intermediate years ranging from roughly $6.2b in 2026 to $14.4b in 2035 in the extended extrapolation.

Discounting those projected cash flows back to today gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $177.83 per share. Against the recent market price of $354.91, the model suggests the stock is about 99.6% above this cash flow based estimate, so on this framework it screens as expensive rather than cheap.

Result: OVERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Applied Materials may be overvalued by 99.6%. Discover 55 high quality undervalued stocks or create your own screener to find better value opportunities.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Applied Materials.

Approach 2: Applied Materials Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Applied Materials, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay for each share to the earnings that support it. It helps you see how many dollars of price the market is assigning to each dollar of current earnings.

What counts as a normal or fair P/E depends heavily on how the market views growth potential and risk. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower multiple.

Applied Materials currently trades on a P/E of 35.94x. That compares with an average P/E of 42.10x for the Semiconductor industry and a peer group average of 52.21x, so the stock sits below both of those reference points. Simply Wall St also calculates a Fair Ratio of 37.60x, which is the P/E it would expect for Applied Materials after accounting for factors such as its earnings growth profile, profit margins, industry, market value and key risks.

This Fair Ratio is more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for company specific characteristics rather than assuming all semiconductor names deserve the same multiple. Against that Fair Ratio, the current P/E of 35.94x is lower, which indicates the shares appear undervalued on this metric.

Result: UNDERVALUED

P/E ratios tell one story, but what if the real opportunity lies elsewhere? Start investing in legacies, not executives. Discover our 23 top founder-led companies.

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Applied Materials Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives. These are simple stories you build around a company like Applied Materials, linking your view on its future revenue, earnings and margins to a forecast and then to a Fair Value. You can then compare this Fair Value with the current price on the Simply Wall St Community page, where Narratives are shared by millions of investors and update automatically when new information such as news or earnings is added. Narratives can differ widely. For example, one Applied Materials Narrative may point to a Fair Value of about US$190.00 while another points to roughly US$420.71. This gives you a clear, visual sense of how different assumptions can lead to very different views on whether the stock looks expensive or attractive on your terms.

For Applied Materials, however, we will make it really easy for you with previews of two leading Applied Materials Narratives:

🐂 Applied Materials Bull Case

Fair value in this narrative: US$398.73

Implied discount or premium vs last close of US$354.91: about 11.0% below that fair value

Assumed annual revenue growth used: 14.32%

  • Analysts in this narrative link Applied Materials' materials engineering and advanced packaging exposure to AI, high performance computing and global fab buildouts, with a focus on a larger addressable market over time.
  • They factor in higher assumed revenue growth, stronger profit margins and a richer future P/E multiple, which together lift their implied fair value estimate compared with earlier models.
  • The story leans on recurring service revenues, a growing installed base and customer co development, while acknowledging risks such as export controls, customer concentration and rising competition.

🐻 Applied Materials Bear Case

Fair value in this narrative: US$194.11

Implied premium vs last close of US$354.91: about 82.9% above that fair value

Assumed annual revenue growth used: 7.0%

  • This narrative still sees AI, data centers and government backed fab expansion as important demand drivers for equipment, but pairs that with reminders that semiconductor spending is cyclical.
  • It anchors on more moderate revenue growth assumptions and a future P/E of 22.0x, while pointing to export controls on China, supply chain issues and geopolitical tensions as meaningful headwinds.
  • The author highlights competition from other major equipment makers and flags that China exposure, capital expenditure swings and policy risk could all weigh on future returns for shareholders.

These two narratives show how different assumptions on growth, risks and the multiple investors might be willing to pay can lead to very different views on what Applied Materials is worth today. They are a useful starting point for you to stress test your own expectations against what other investors are currently thinking about the stock.

data.title: How O’Reilly’s 2025 Results, 2026 Outlook, and Buybacks Will Impact O’Reilly Automotive (ORLY) Investors
data.description:

data.title: Romania NuScale SMR And AI Fuel Deal Shape Execution Outlook
data.description:
• Romania has approved the final investment decision for Europe's first NuScale (NYSE:SMR) small modular reactor power plant.
  • The project will start with one of six planned SMR units, which is intended to serve as a reference for future expansion.
  • NuScale also announced a collaboration with Oak Ridge National Laboratory to apply artificial intelligence to nuclear fuel management across multiple reactors.

NuScale Power, listed as NYSE:SMR, focuses on small modular reactors designed to provide grid scale, low carbon baseload electricity. The Romanian project is the first commercial deployment of its SMR design in the European Union, adding a real world reference to a sector where many projects are still on paper. At the same time, the AI work with Oak Ridge National Laboratory targets core nuclear operating questions such as fuel use and reactor performance.

For investors watching nuclear power as an energy transition theme, these two updates relate to both deployment and technology development. The Romanian plant offers a potential proof point for NuScale's SMR model, while the AI partnership could influence how efficiently future fleets are run. Together, they provide more concrete milestones to track when assessing how NuScale executes on its commercial plans.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for NuScale Power by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on NuScale Power.

📰 Beyond the headline: 3 risks and 2 things going right for NuScale Power that every investor should see.

For NuScale, Romania’s final investment decision and the Oak Ridge AI partnership both relate to execution of its business model rather than new concepts on a slide deck. The Doicesti project moves a long-discussed 6 unit plant into an initial build phase with one 77 MW unit, which gives NuScale a path to demonstrate construction, licensing, and operation at commercial scale in the EU. That is important in a field where peers such as Rolls Royce, GE Hitachi, and TerraPower are also working to secure first of a kind deployments. In parallel, the AI fuel management work with Oak Ridge, backed by the U.S. Department of Energy, relates to NuScale’s indication that a multi module plant can use a shared fuel pool to manage costs more efficiently. If the research achieves practical results, it could contribute to the operating case for future 12 module sites, including power for energy intensive users such as data centers.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The NuScale Power Narrative

  • The Romanian FID ties into the narrative’s focus on deployment visibility, giving NuScale a clearer reference project for the RoPower plant and supporting the idea of future commercial revenue from SMR contracts.
  • The staged approach in Romania and references to extended timelines highlight the execution and timing risks already raised in the narrative, especially around when contracts translate into substantial cash flow.
  • The AI fuel management partnership, and the potential for multi reactor fuel cost savings, is not fully reflected in the existing narrative, which focuses more on orders and government support than on long term operating efficiency levers.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for NuScale Power to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Execution risk around the Romanian project, including the test first approach and extended timelines, could delay when NuScale sees meaningful revenue and may affect perceptions of SMR rollout speed.
  • ⚠️ NuScale is still unprofitable and analysts do not expect profitability in the next 3 years, so funding needs and potential share dilution remain key issues to watch.
  • 🎁 Revenue is forecast to grow 47.75% per year, which indicates that analysts see significant top line expansion potential as projects such as Doicesti and other agreements move forward.
  • 🎁 The shares are described as trading at 63.6% below one estimate of fair value, which suggests some investors may view current pricing as leaving room for upside if execution improves.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, the key items to track are concrete milestones on the Romanian site, such as contract awards, regulatory steps, and construction progress for the first 77 MW module. You may also want to watch how the AI fuel management work with Oak Ridge translates into quantifiable targets for fuel cost savings or operating flexibility at a full 12 module configuration. Any new agreements or updates with other counterparties, such as ENTRA1 Energy or utilities considering SMRs, will influence the outlook for future orders and cash flow. Finally, monitor NuScale’s balance sheet, funding sources, and any further commentary from analysts about timelines and risk sharing on large projects.
